title: Viennese biscuits
subtitle: Piped butter biscuits, dipped in dark chocolate
excerpt: Viennese biscuits are piped butter biscuits with a very short, melt-in-the-mouth texture, made from a soft dough of butter, icing sugar, and flour. They are piped in a W or spiral shape, baked pale, then one end dipped in dark chocolate. The high butter ratio makes the dough too soft to handle; it must be piped directly from the bag.
sourceNotes: Viennese biscuits (Wiener Kipferl and related forms) draw on Viennese pastry traditions documented from the nineteenth century. The British version (piped, star-nozzled, chocolate-dipped) is a twentieth-century adaptation that appears in British domestic baking books from the mid-century onward. Firmly public domain.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin for up to 4 days. The chocolate sets firm in a cool room.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; they should stay pale, not colour

content.text: Viennese biscuits are piped from a very soft butter dough through a star nozzle, giving the characteristic ribbed surface. They are baked at a low temperature to stay pale, then one end is dipped in chocolate once cool. The dough has a very high butter content and cannot be rolled or shaped by hand; the piping bag is not optional.

Beat the very soft butter and icing sugar together until pale and very fluffy. Add the vanilla. Sift in the flour and cornflour and beat until just combined. The dough should be soft enough to pipe without forcing; if it is too firm, the ridges from the star nozzle won't hold.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Line baking trays with baking paper. Fill a piping bag fitted with a large star nozzle and pipe W shapes or small rosettes onto the tray, spaced 4 cm apart. Bake for 14 to 16 minutes. The biscuits are done when they are just set and very pale gold at the edges; they should not colour on the surface. Cool on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring to a wire rack.
Melt the dark chocolate in a bowl over hot water. Dip one end of each cooled biscuit and lay on baking paper to set. Leave at room temperature in a cool kitchen.
